topic: Production ML Inference Platform — Triton, MLflow, FastAPI

constraint_log.constraint_text: Before any function loads a model version or fetches artifacts, log to MLflow using mlflow.log_param(...) or mlflow.set_tag(...).
All inference API endpoints must include a model_version field in the JSON response body.

constraint_log.old_constraint_text: Before any function loads a model version or fetches artifacts, log to MLflow using mlflow.log_param(...) or mlflow.set_tag(...).
constraint_log.new_constraint_text: Keep the existing MLflow param/tag logging before model load or artifact fetch, and additionally log inference latency on every inference call using mlflow.log_metric("latency_ms", value).

constraint_log.constraint_text: All async tasks that interact with the model registry (load, reload, version switch) must acquire a distributed lock using async with registry_lock: before proceeding.
All inference API endpoints must include a model_version field in the JSON response body.

turns.text: I am designing a production ML inference platform. Can you sketch the core architecture with Python services?

Use FastAPI as the request gateway, Triton Inference Server for model execution, MLflow for experiment/model registry, a feature store for online features, and Prometheus plus Grafana for observability. Put FastAPI behind an API gateway, keep Triton in a separate deployment, and add a lightweight orchestration service for model promotion and rollback.
How should we store model artifacts and choose between Triton and TorchServe?

Store artifacts in object storage with immutable versioned paths and register metadata in MLflow Model Registry. Given your need for multi-framework support, dynamic batching, and GPU scheduling, Triton is the better fit than TorchServe; we can still keep FastAPI as the business API facade.
Our team standardized on MLflow, so every model load needs to log to it before use.

Understood. I will enforce that by logging before load, for example: def load_model(alias): mlflow.set_tag("load_alias", alias); model_uri = client.get_model_version_by_alias("fraud_model", alias).source; return triton_loader.load(model_uri).
Great. What model versioning strategy should we use for dev, staging, and prod?

Use MLflow aliases and stages together: alias dev_candidate, staging_candidate, and prod_champion. Promotion should be metadata-only first, then rollout via canary in FastAPI routing, with rollback to prior alias on SLO breach.
Can you define an artifact URI scheme that keeps lineage clean?

Use a deterministic URI pattern like s3://ml-artifacts/{model_name}/{run_id}/{flavor}/. In any fetch function, log first: mlflow.log_param("artifact_uri", uri); mlflow.set_tag("artifact_stage", "staging"); local_path = mlflow.artifacts.download_artifacts(uri).
How do we connect experiment runs to serving releases?

When promoting a run, copy run_id, git_sha, data_snapshot_id into model version tags. Then FastAPI can emit release metadata to logs so we can trace prediction outcomes back to the exact training run.
Also, downstream services are expecting a model_version field in every response body.

Noted. Endpoint example: @app.post("/predict") async def predict(req): mlflow.set_tag("endpoint", "predict"); version = current_model_version(); pred = infer(req); return {"prediction": pred, "model_version": version}. This keeps the response contract explicit.
Let us move to serving optimization. How should we tune Triton dynamic batching?

Start with max_batch_size 64, preferred_batch_size [8,16,32], and max_queue_delay_microseconds around 2000-4000. Measure p95 latency versus throughput and tune queue delay based on your SLO; this follows our Triton-first decision from turn 4.
What about GPU allocation for multiple models?

Use Triton instance_group with explicit GPU affinity and separate model instances for heavy versus lightweight models. If you have A100s, consider MIG partitions so fraud model and fallback model do not starve each other.
Can we add warm-up so first request latency is lower?

Yes. On startup, call a warmup loader that logs before load: mlflow.set_tag("warmup_model", "fraud_prod"); mlflow.log_param("loaded_version", version); model = load_model(version). Then send synthetic infer requests and keep endpoint responses as {"prediction": sample_pred, "model_version": version, "latency_budget_ms": 120}.
How would you run canary deployment for a new model version?

Route 5% traffic to candidate_v2 and 95% to champion_v1 using a deterministic user hash. Compare business KPIs plus p95 latency and error rate; auto-promote only after minimum sample size and stable metrics for 24 hours.
What rollback trigger thresholds should we set?

Trigger rollback if candidate p95 latency exceeds champion by 20% for 15 minutes, or if fraud recall drops by more than 1.5 absolute points. Keep a one-click alias revert in MLflow and health-based route switch in FastAPI.
Please share a small FastAPI gateway skeleton for Triton calls.

Use a startup loader: mlflow.log_param("gateway_model_alias", "prod_champion"); version = resolve_alias("prod_champion"); triton_client.load_model(version). In /predict, return {"prediction": pred, "score": score, "model_version": version}.
How should we cache loaded models and avoid repeated registry calls?

Use an in-memory cache keyed by model alias and version, with TTL plus ETag checks from registry metadata. On cache miss, log MLflow param or tag before artifact fetch, then populate cache and emit cache_miss metric.
What is a safe fallback strategy when Triton times out?

Implement timeout with circuit breaker: try Triton for 120 ms, else call fallback model. Before fallback load, do mlflow.set_tag("fallback_attempt", "true"); fallback = load_model("fraud_prod"). Return {"prediction": pred, "score": score, "source": "fallback"}.
Can we add retries and deadlines per request class?

Yes. Define classes like realtime and bulk with separate timeout budgets and retry policy. Realtime should be no retry, bulk can retry once with jitter; response remains {"prediction": pred, "model_version": version, "request_class": cls}.
How do we propagate trace IDs across FastAPI and Triton?

Inject x-trace-id at ingress and pass it as Triton request metadata, then log trace_id with model_version and run_id in structured logs. This makes debugging canary issues easier when comparing champion and candidate behavior.
What autoscaling signals should drive FastAPI and Triton replicas?

Scale FastAPI on CPU and request concurrency, and Triton on GPU utilization, queue delay, and model execution count. Keep scale-up aggressive and scale-down conservative to avoid cold starts that hurt your warm-up strategy from turn 20.
Should we use Triton model control mode explicit or poll?
Use explicit mode for production so promotion is controlled by your release pipeline. Poll mode is fine in lower environments but can introduce unplanned reloads that complicate incident response.
Can you propose startup order for services?

Startup order: registry and object storage checks, then FastAPI config load, then Triton health, then model preload with mlflow.log_param("startup_alias", "prod_champion") and mlflow.set_tag("startup_phase", "preload"), then open traffic. A startup validation endpoint can return {"prediction": test_pred, "model_version": loaded_version}.
Moving on, how should feature store integration work for online inference?
Use online feature retrieval with strict timeout and default fallback values. Include feature_vector_version in logs, and keep online transformations aligned with training definitions to avoid skew.
What should our request preprocessing pipeline include?

Do schema validation, type coercion, missing value imputation, categorical mapping, and feature scaling in a deterministic order. Version the preprocessing bundle and tie it to the model version at release time.
How do we ensure online and offline feature parity?
Maintain one transformation spec and generate both batch and online code from it, then run daily parity checks on sampled records. Keep parity drift alerting as a blocker for promotions.
MLflow standard update: every inference call now also needs mlflow.log_metric("latency_ms", value), in addition to pre-load param/tag logging.

Acknowledged. I will keep pre-load logging and add per-call latency metric: mlflow.log_param("model_alias", alias); model = load_model(alias); t0 = now_ms(); pred = infer(req); t1 = now_ms(); mlflow.log_metric("latency_ms", t1 - t0); return {"prediction": pred, "model_version": alias_to_version(alias)}.
Now let us design A/B testing for model variants.

Use deterministic bucketing by user_id and route to A or B. Before loading each variant, record mlflow.set_tag("ab_variant", variant) and mlflow.log_param("model_alias", alias); for each inference record mlflow.log_metric("latency_ms", latency). Return {"prediction": pred, "model_version": chosen_version, "variant": variant}.
How do we configure traffic split safely?
Store split config in a dynamic config service with versioned rollout plans, for example 90/10 then 70/30 then 50/50. Add guardrails so split progression halts automatically on SLO regressions.
What statistical checks should gate promotion?

Use two-sided hypothesis tests for core KPI deltas, sequential monitoring correction, and minimum detectable effect planning. Combine significance with practical thresholds so tiny but significant changes do not trigger noisy decisions.
How can we prevent a bad variant from harming users during test?
Set hard stop rules: latency, error rate, and business KPI floors. If any hard stop breaks, route all traffic to champion immediately and mark variant as blocked in registry metadata.
Do we need user-level stickiness?
Yes, sticky assignment is essential to avoid cross-contamination and unstable metrics. Use consistent hashing on user_id and persist assignment for the experiment duration.
Can you show a concrete endpoint sample for the experiment flow?

Sample flow: pick bucket, map to candidate alias, do mlflow.set_tag("experiment", "ab_fraud_q2"); mlflow.log_param("candidate", alias); pred = predict(payload); return {"prediction": pred, "model_version": selected_version, "bucket": bucket}.
How should we audit experiment decisions in MLflow?

Create one parent run per experiment and child runs per variant/day, logging assignment policy, thresholds, and stop reasons. Keep per-inference latency logging via mlflow.log_metric("latency_ms", value) and tags for variant, region, and release id.
Could we use multi-armed bandits later?

Yes, but start with fixed-split A/B for explainability and auditability, then graduate to contextual bandits once your reward signal is stable. Keep a hard exploration cap and safe fallback to champion.
What kill switch design do you recommend for emergency disable?
Implement a global feature flag checked at request ingress; if enabled, force champion-only routing. Also have per-variant kill switches and a signed override path for incident commanders.
Let us discuss shadow mode inference before full rollout.

Run challenger in shadow for mirrored traffic, store predictions asynchronously, and never use shadow output for user response. Compare shadow versus champion over the same request IDs to estimate real-world impact.
How do we decide champion-challenger promotions?

Promotion gate should combine KPI uplift, latency, and stability checks. For each inference call in evaluation, keep mlflow.log_metric("latency_ms", latency), and return {"decision": "hold_or_promote", "model_version": challenger_version, "shadow_score": score}; this aligns with your earlier canary and rollback criteria.
We had a race condition last week during model reload, and my lead now requires distributed lock usage for all async registry operations.

Agreed, we should enforce lock discipline. Example: async def switch_version(alias): async with registry_lock: mlflow.log_param("switch_alias", alias); version = await registry.get_by_alias(alias); await registry.activate(version). This directly addresses the reload incident you mentioned.
Now drift detection: which signals should we track first?

Track feature distribution drift, prediction distribution drift, concept drift proxies from delayed labels, and segment-level drift by region/product. Combine statistical and business thresholds so alerts are meaningful.
What thresholds do you suggest for PSI and KS?

A practical starting point is PSI > 0.2 warning and > 0.3 critical; KS statistic threshold can be tuned per feature family but start with 0.1 warning and 0.2 critical. Calibrate by backtesting on stable historical windows.
How should the monitoring dashboard be organized?

Use four panels: system SLOs, model quality, data drift, and experiment status. Include drill-down by model_version and by cohort so on-call can isolate whether regressions are infra, data, or model related.
Can you provide an async drift-triggered reload example?

Sure: async def reload_model_if_drifted(score): if score > 0.3: mlflow.log_param("reload_target", "staging_candidate"); mlflow.set_tag("drift_trigger", "psi"); v = await registry.get_latest("fraud_model"); await registry.set_alias("staging_candidate", v). For inference after reload, measure and log mlflow.log_metric("latency_ms", latency); return {"status": "reloaded", "model_version": active_version, "drift_score": score}.
Given the race incident, can you harden that reload snippet?

Yes, lock it explicitly: async def reload_model_if_drifted(score): if score > 0.3: async with registry_lock: mlflow.log_param("reload_target", "staging_candidate"); v = await registry.get_latest("fraud_model"); await registry.set_alias("staging_candidate", v). Keep inference-side mlflow.log_metric("latency_ms", latency) unchanged.
What retraining trigger policy works with these drift signals?

Use a tiered policy: warning creates investigation tickets, critical sustained drift for N windows triggers retraining pipeline, and severe business KPI drop triggers immediate retrain plus rollback review. This builds on your dashboard and threshold setup from turns 76 to 78.
How do we run root-cause analysis when drift alerts fire?

Start with segmentation by geography, channel, and merchant type, then compare feature importances and data quality checks against baseline windows. Correlate with release events to separate model drift from upstream data pipeline issues.
What data quality monitors should feed retraining decisions?

Monitor null ratios, schema violations, late-arriving features, cardinality explosions, and label delay distributions. Feed these into a retraining readiness score so you do not retrain on degraded data.
Platform team now injects model_version via middleware, so individual endpoints no longer need to include that field manually.

Understood, we can remove endpoint-level model_version boilerplate and rely on middleware. For CI/CD, keep mlflow.log_param("pipeline_stage", "promote") and mlflow.set_tag("cicd", "true") before registry actions, wrap async version switches in async with registry_lock:, and keep mlflow.log_metric("latency_ms", latency) on every inference path.
Please outline CI/CD steps for model retraining to deployment.

Pipeline: data validation, training, evaluation, bias checks, registration, canary deploy, and post-deploy monitoring gates. Any async registry promotion or rollback stage must run under async with registry_lock:, and inference services continue per-call latency metric logging.
How would you combine blue-green rollout with cost controls?

Run green at reduced capacity first, then ramp while monitoring cost per 1k predictions and GPU utilization. Use scheduled downscaling for off-peak, mixed precision where accurate, and keep rollback path instant via locked registry alias switch.
Can you define retraining pipeline orchestration details?

Use an orchestrator DAG with tasks for extract, validate, train, evaluate, register, and deploy. During deploy task, log MLflow params/tags before any model fetch, and for async registry load or switch do async with registry_lock: to avoid concurrent promotion races.
I also need practical cost governance policies.

Set per-team monthly budgets, enforce cost anomaly alerts, and define SLO-aware autoscaling caps. Track unit economics such as cost per prediction and require approval when experiments exceed predefined GPU-hour budgets.
Final review please: summarize the architecture and safeguards we agreed on.

Final design: FastAPI gateway fronts Triton, MLflow handles tracking and registry, feature store feeds online inference, and observability spans SLO, drift, and business KPIs. We kept Triton over TorchServe from the early decision, preserved MLflow pre-load param/tag logging from turn 5, added per-inference mlflow.log_metric("latency_ms", value) after the update at turn 47, enforced async with registry_lock: for registry load/reload/switch after the incident callout at turn 71, and moved model_version injection to middleware per turn 89. This gives controlled rollout, drift-aware retraining, and cost-governed operations with clear rollback paths.
